old_claim.note: Laser scanners are common in modern sorting systems, but sorting can be achieved with non-laser optical and mechanical workflows.

new_claim.note: The project compared pattern-recognition methods and reported neural networks as the best classifier for its NIR spectra.

source_shape.source_locator: The 1993-1996 project describes robotic pickup, NIR and MIR sensing, digital spectral preprocessing, pattern recognition including neural networks, and software-controlled mechanical or pneumatic sorting of plastics.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The CORDIS project used NIR and MIR sensors, digital images, pattern recognition, and neural networks to classify plastic for automated sorting.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Pattern-recognition software is directly part of the documented classifier, while the general NIR design used an infrared camera and monochromator rather than requiring a laser scanner.

ontology_before: Advanced recycling sortation treated laser scanning as its general optical prerequisite and omitted the documented classifier lineage.
ontology_after: Robotic plastics sortation depends on robotics, polymer-signature knowledge, and early machine-learning methods.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-12-launch-source-check-completion: advanced_recycling_sortation now depends on machine_learning_early_algorithms and no longer on laser_scanners.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed to a laser-only Raman sorting system.
A source shows the 1993 NIR sorting workflow did not use pattern recognition or neural networks.
short_reason: machine_learning_early_algorithms directly supports the scoped system; laser_scanners does not.
